turns.content: I just started learning Rust and I have a failing CI/CD pipeline that seems to be related to my custom macros. Can you help me understand how to report errors properly in a proc macro?

That is an excellent starting point, but let us narrow it down first. In Rust, when writing a proc macro, the way you construct and return error messages directly impacts whether your compiler can provide helpful diagnostics to users of your crate. The core mechanism for this involves using the syn library to parse tokens and then creating errors with specific span information so that the error message points exactly where the problem occurred in the user's source code rather than inside the macro itself.
So if I want to catch a mistake in my input, how do I actually generate an error that the compiler understands?

You should create a syn::Error object using the new_spanned constructor which takes a span and a message. This allows you to attach the location of the problem directly into your error structure so that when you return it, the compiler can highlight the exact line in the user's code instead of pointing back at your macro definition.
And what if I need multiple errors? Do I just stack them?

Yes, but there is a specific pattern for that. You can use the append method on syn::Error to add more diagnostics without creating a new error object each time. This keeps all your related problems together in one structure rather than scattering them across multiple compile errors which would confuse the user.
I am still confused about how I actually return this from my macro function so it becomes visible to the compiler?

You must ensure your proc macro implementation returns an Option<syn::Error> or a Result type that carries syn::Error. When you do this, Rust will treat any non-None value as a compilation error and stop processing the file. This is how the compiler converts your internal diagnostics into actual errors it can display to the user.
Is there anything else I should know about handling these errors?

You might want to consider using the proc_macro_error crate which wraps syn::Error and provides helper functions for common error patterns. This can make your code more readable by abstracting away some of the boilerplate required to construct proper error structures with spans.
Okay, so my pipeline was failing because I wasn't using new_spanned correctly?

It is very likely. If your macro returns errors without proper span information or if you are not returning syn::Error at all, the compiler will generate generic messages that do not point to specific locations in the user's code. This makes debugging extremely difficult and often causes CI/CD pipelines to fail silently or with misleading error messages.
I'm looking at this error: my macro expansion failed with a span pointing to the wrong line. I tried using syn::Error::new_spanned but it seems to be returning compile_error! at the wrong location.

The issue is likely that you're creating an Error node without attaching it to a proper Span. When you use syn::Error::new_spanned, you need to ensure the span context matches exactly where the diagnostic occurred in the source file.
That makes sense, but I thought proc_macro_error crate handles all of this automatically. Shouldn't I just use that instead and not worry about spans manually?

The proc_macro_error crate does simplify error creation by wrapping syn::Error internally, but you still need to understand how it maps diagnostics to source spans. It accumulates errors properly though, which is different from what you were trying to do before.

Wait, I see the problem now. I was returning compile_error! directly without checking if my error list had multiple items yet. The proc_macro_error crate actually helps with that multi-error accumulation case.

Exactly. When you have multiple errors to report, using syn::Error::new_spanned alone isn't enough because it only holds a single error node. You need to wrap those in the proc_macro_error crate so they get properly formatted and accumulated.
So I should be collecting all my diagnostic messages first, then wrapping them into one syn::Error object before returning it from my macro?
Yes. You build a list of individual errors using the crate's helper methods, pass that collection to syn::Error::new_spanned with the correct span, and then return that as your final error node.
But what about the Span trait? I need to make sure my code implements that so the compiler knows where exactly the error happened?

The proc_macro_error crate already provides the necessary implementation details for you. You don't need to manually implement the Span trait or figure out how to attach spans unless you're building something beyond basic diagnostics.

I'm stuck on this exercise from the book where I need to parse a config file and report errors back to the compiler. My current approach uses syn::Error with new_spanned, but when I try to return compile_error! it seems to lose the span information or just prints generic messages instead of pointing exactly where the syntax error was in the source code.

That's a classic frustration when moving from basic parsing to proper diagnostics. The issue is that syn::Error::new_spanned creates a diagnostic with a span, but compile_error! macro doesn't automatically respect that span unless you explicitly pass it or wrap it correctly. You're probably getting generic error messages because the compiler isn't seeing the actual location of the problem in your source code.

The issue is that syn::Error::new_spanned creates a diagnostic with a span, but compile_error! macro doesn't automatically respect that span unless you explicitly pass it or wrap it correctly. You're probably getting generic error messages because the compiler isn't seeing the actual location of the problem in your source code.

I'm stuck on this exercise from the book where I need to parse a config file and report errors back to the compiler. My current approach uses syn::Error with new_spanned, but when I try to return compile_error! it seems to lose the span information or just prints generic messages instead of pointing exactly where the syntax error was in the source code.
So maybe I should be using proc_macro_error crate instead? I've seen it mentioned in other crates and it sounds like it handles multi-error accumulation better than my current single-error approach.

You're on the right track. The proc_macro_error crate is specifically designed for this scenario where you need to collect multiple errors during parsing or transformation. It wraps syn::Error internally but provides a cleaner interface that automatically accumulates errors and formats them nicely when returned via the macro. This avoids the complexity of manually managing spans and error lists yourself.
So maybe I should be using proc_macro_error crate instead? I've seen it mentioned in other crates and it sounds like it handles multi-error accumulation better than my current single-error approach.

You're on the right track. The proc_macro_error crate is specifically designed for this scenario where you need to collect multiple errors during parsing or transformation. It wraps syn::Error internally but provides a cleaner interface that automatically accumulates errors and formats them nicely when returned via the macro. This avoids the complexity of manually managing spans and error lists yourself.

That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how to actually integrate it into my derive or attribute macro code. Does it replace syn::Error entirely or do I need to keep using that under the hood? And what exactly does compile_error! receive when used with this crate?

The proc_macro_error crate doesn't replace syn::Error; rather, it's a wrapper that builds upon syn::Error. Under the hood you'll still be creating syn::Diagnostic objects and using new_spanned, but the crate handles the accumulation logic for you. When you call compile_error! with a message string, the crate takes that string along with any associated span information from your syn diagnostics and formats it into a proper rustc_diagnostic_surface output that the compiler can understand.

That makes sense, but I'm still confused about how to actually integrate it into my derive or attribute macro code. Does it replace syn::Error entirely or do I need to keep using that under the hood? And what exactly does compile_error! receive when used with this crate?

The proc_macro_error crate doesn't replace syn::Error; rather, it's a wrapper that builds upon syn::Error. Under the hood you'll still be creating syn::Diagnostic objects and using new_spanned, but the crate handles the accumulation logic for you. When you call compile_error! with a message string, the crate takes that string along with any associated span information from your syn diagnostics and formats it into a proper rustc_diagnostic_surface output that the compiler can understand.
Okay so I need to import proc_macro_error and maybe use its Error type? And can you explain why just passing a simple String to compile_error! isn't enough when building my own parser logic?

You do need to import proc_macro_error and use its Error type which implements the std::error::Error trait properly for rustc. The reason a plain String isn't sufficient is because rustc's error handling system expects structured diagnostic data including location spans, severity levels, and potentially multiple related errors. Without this structure, your parser won't be able to provide meaningful context about where in the code the problem occurred.

You do need to import proc_macro_error and use its Error type which implements the std::error::Error trait properly for rustc. The reason a plain String isn't sufficient is because rustc's error handling system expects structured diagnostic data including location spans, severity levels, and potentially multiple related errors. Without this structure, your parser won't be able to provide meaningful context about where in the code the problem occurred.

Got it, so I'm building up a list of diagnostics as I parse tokens, then calling compile_error! once with all of them? Is there anything else I should consider like error recovery or suggesting fixes?

Yes, exactly. As you iterate through the token stream using syn::Parser traits, you accumulate individual errors into a vec<syn::Error> or similar structure. Then you pass that collection to proc_macro_error's compile_error macro which formats it all together. For advanced tooling like this, you might also want to consider suggesting recovery points or possible fixes by analyzing the syntax tree and using features from crates like rustc_span or custom heuristics in your parser.

Then you pass that collection to proc_macro_error's compile_error macro which formats it all together. For advanced tooling like this, you might also want to consider suggesting recovery points or possible fixes by analyzing the syntax tree and using features from crates like rustc_span or custom heuristics in your parser.
One last thing - how do I handle cases where there are no errors at all? The book says I should return Ok(()) but I'm worried about mixing error paths with success paths cleanly.

That's a great question about the control flow. When your parser successfully processes everything without encountering any diagnostics, you simply don't call compile_error! at all and instead return Ok(something) from your macro function. The proc_macro_error crate only activates when errors are present; if there's nothing to report, just use standard Rust patterns for success cases like returning the parsed AST or configuration data.

Perfect, that clarifies it. So my flow is accumulate syn::Diagnostics -> check if any exist -> if yes pass to proc_macro_error compile_error! -> else return Ok(parsed_data). Thanks for walking me through this!

You're very welcome! That's a solid understanding of the pattern. Just remember that while you're accumulating errors, make sure each one retains its span information from when it was first created during parsing. That's what allows the final error message to point precisely to the problematic location in your source code rather than just being a generic string.
